title: Navarin d'Agneau
subtitle: French spring lamb stew with new vegetables
sourceNotes: Navarin d'agneau printanier is a canonical dish of French spring cookery, codified by Escoffier in Le Guide Culinaire (1903). The word navarin comes from navet (turnip), a defining ingredient of the dish. Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) contains a closely related recipe.

content.text: is lighter than a bourguignon. The vegetables go in for the final 35 minutes, not cooked separately and added at the end. Too early and they collapse. At the right point they soak up the lamb juices and stay tender while keeping their shape.
Brown the lamb and build the braise

Heat the oven to 160 °C fan. Season {{lamb-neck-fillet}} of lamb and dust with flour. Heat butter and oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown the lamb in batches for 4 to 5 minutes until deeply coloured. Remove. Cook the onion in the remaining fat for 5 minutes, add the garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute, then pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine. Add {{lamb-stock}} of stock, crushed tomatoes, and a
. Return the lamb, bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven for 45 minutes.

Remove from the oven. Add {{new-potatoes}} of new potatoes, {{baby-carrot}} of baby carrots, and {{turnip}} of turnip wedges, pushing them into the liquid. Return to the oven uncovered for 30 to 35 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the lamb is falling from the bone.
Remove the bouquet garni. Stir in {{frozen-peas}} of peas and leave for 2 to 3 minutes in the hot stew. Taste and adjust seasoning, scatter with parsley, and serve from the casserole.
